entities.name: John Deacon
entities.description: A clergyman who wrote 'Dialogicall Discourses of ... Devils' (1601).

entities.name: John Walker
entities.description: A clergyman who wrote 'Dialogicall Discourses of ... Devils' (1601).

entities.name: Sir Roger Manwood
entities.description: Knight and Lord Chief Baron of the King's Court of the Exchequer, recipient of Scot's Epistle.

entities.name: Wier
entities.description: An author whose views on demonology were influential during Scot's time.

entities.name: Cornelius Agrippa
entities.description: An author whose views on demonology were influential during Scot's time.

entities.name: Hippocrates
entities.description: Ancient physician cited for opinions on magical cures.

entities.name: Calvin
entities.description: Reformer who referred to magicians as 'cousenors'.

entities.name: Erastus
entities.description: A writer who defended certain aspects of magic, such as flying in the air.

entities.name: Devils and Spirits
entities.description: Entities that witches are alleged to contract with or use for purposes.

entities.name: Witchcraft
entities.description: The practice of using magic or spirits to cause harm or perform feats.

entities.name: Charms, Philtres, Periapts, Pentacles, Curses, and Conjurations
entities.description: Methods through which magic is alleged to work.

summary: This passage provides a biographical and critical overview of Reginald Scot's 'The Discoverie of Witchcraft', highlighting his skeptical stance against the superstitions of magic, conjuration, and witchcraft. It details historical connections between authors, discusses the identity of the author through legal documents, and presents an epistle addressed to Sir Roger Manwood advocating for justice for those accused of witchcraft.
